data.title: ComfortDelGro (SGX:C52) Earnings Growth Outpaces Modest Revenue Trend And Tests Value Narrative
data.description:
ComfortDelGro (SGX:C52) has put fresh numbers on the table for FY 2025, with second half revenue at S$2.6b, basic EPS of S$0.057, and net income of S$124.3m. Trailing twelve month revenue reached S$5.1b with EPS at S$0.106. Over recent periods the company has reported revenue of S$2.4b and EPS of S$0.053 in the second half of 2024, followed by S$2.4b and S$0.049 in the first half of 2025, and then S$2.6b and S$0.057 in the latest half. Together, these figures contribute to a mixed but serviceable margin picture for investors to assess.

See our full analysis for ComfortDelGro.

With the headline figures in place, the next step is to set these results against the most common narratives around ComfortDelGro to see which storylines the numbers support and which ones they start to question.

Earnings Growth Outruns Modest Revenue Trend

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue is cited at S$5.1b while earnings grew 9.4% with a five year average of 15.3% per year, which is stronger than the 3.8% annual revenue growth forecast.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that reported earnings growth of 9.4% and a five year track record of 15.3% per year sit alongside a more modest 3.8% revenue growth forecast, which means:
    • Supporters can point to earnings momentum that is also expected to run ahead of the wider Singapore market, with forecast earnings growth of 6.4% compared to a 4.8% market forecast.
    • Cautious investors may still watch how this gap between earnings growth and slower revenue expectations holds up if cost pressures or contract terms shift.

Margins Sit At 4.6% With Slight Compression

  • The latest net profit margin is 4.6%, described as slightly below last year’s 4.7%, against trailing twelve month net income of S$230.3m on S$5.1b of revenue.
  • Critics focus on the bearish angle that a 4.6% margin, sitting just under last year’s 4.7%, leaves less room for error, yet:
    • The same period still produced that 9.4% earnings growth, showing that, so far, the small margin movement has not prevented higher overall profits in absolute terms.
    • The combination of steady margins around the mid single digit level and earnings growth gives both sides numbers to point to when they debate how durable profitability could be.

Valuation Gap And Dividend Coverage Tension

  • At a share price of S$1.45, ComfortDelGro is reported to be trading about 37.8% below a DCF fair value of roughly S$2.33, with a P/E of 13.6x versus a 20.5x peer average and a 5.86% dividend yield that is not well covered by free cash flow.

    • On one side, the below peer P/E of 13.6x and the 37.8% gap to DCF fair value are often used to support a value angle around the shares.
    • On the other, the 5.86% dividend being flagged as not well covered by free cash flow gives cautious investors a clear metric to monitor if cash generation softens or capital needs rise.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Boot Barn Holdings (BOOT) After Recent Price Volatility?
data.description:

  • The stock closed at US$152.83, with returns of 42.5% over 1 year and 104.3% over 3 years, while the past 30 days show a 21.9% decline and the year-to-date return sits at an 18.1% decline.

Boot Barn Holdings scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: Boot Barn Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s dollars to arrive at an intrinsic value per share. It is essentially asking what Boot Barn Holdings’ future cash generation is worth right now.

For Boot Barn Holdings, the latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about US$97.4 million. Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model that incorporates analyst forecasts where available, then extends the series. For example, projected Free Cash Flow for 2028 is US$59.6 million, and there are further extrapolated cash flow estimates running out to 2035, all in US$.

After discounting these projected cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated fair value of US$31.28 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$152.83, this implies the stock is very expensive on this DCF view, with an intrinsic discount suggesting it is around 388.5% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Boot Barn Holdings.

Approach 2: Boot Barn Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually the main driver of long term returns.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends on what investors expect from the company and how much risk they see. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth and higher risk usually point to a lower one.

Boot Barn Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 21.23x. That sits above the Specialty Retail industry average of 19.50x and the peer average of 14.11x. To go a step further, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary "Fair Ratio" for Boot Barn Holdings of 18.30x, which reflects factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific characteristics instead of assuming one size fits all. Comparing the current P/E of 21.23x with the Fair Ratio of 18.30x suggests the shares are trading at a premium.

Result: OVERVALUED

data.description:

  • Critics highlight a weaker margin profile as a bearish signal, and the numbers give them specific pressure points to reference:
    • The step down from 17.2% to 12% margin sits alongside trailing twelve month revenue ranging from about ¥1.58b to ¥2.16b. This means the issue is how much profit Carote keeps on each yuan of sales rather than whether sales exist.

  • On a trailing basis, basic EPS is HK$0.0893, compared with HK$0.0956 in the prior trailing window, while the latest half year printed HK$0.0449 of EPS.
  • Bears focus on the 33.5% per year earnings decline over the past five years, and these EPS figures line up with that concern:

data.title: Is It Time To Reconsider Mastercard (MA) Around US$500 After Mixed Multi Year Returns
data.description:
• Wondering if Mastercard at around US$500 a share is still a quality name at a fair price, or if you would be overpaying for growth that may already be reflected in the stock.
  • The share price closed at US$500.75, with a 2.0% return over the last 7 days, 0.6% over the last 30 days, a year-to-date return of an 11.1% decline, a 9.7% decline over 1 year, and a 41.8% return over both 3 and 5 years.
  • These mixed returns can reflect shifting views on growth potential and risk, with some investors focusing on shorter term pullbacks while others look at multi-year gains. Recent coverage has centered on how global payment volumes and digital transaction trends could shape expectations for Mastercard over time.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives Mastercard a valuation score of 3 out of 6, so next up is a closer look at what different valuation methods say about the stock, and why there may be an even better way to make sense of those valuation signals by the end of this article.

Mastercard delivered -9.7% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Diversified Financial industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Mastercard Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company can generate above the return that shareholders require, then capitalizes those extra earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Mastercard, the starting point is a Book Value of US$8.65 per share and a Stable EPS estimate of US$25.23 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 12 analysts. Against a Cost of Equity of US$1.06 per share, this implies an Excess Return of US$24.16 per share, which is the earnings attributed to shareholders after covering their required return.

The model also uses a Stable Book Value of US$14.65 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 6 analysts, and an average Return on Equity described as very large at roughly 1.7 times equity. Putting these inputs together, the Excess Returns framework arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$644.05 per share.

Compared with the recent share price around US$500.75, this Excess Returns valuation suggests Mastercard trades at roughly a 22.2% discount, which screens as attractive on this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Mastercard is undervalued by 22.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 61 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: Mastercard Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Mastercard, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It helps you quickly compare what the market is willing to pay for one business versus another that is also generating profits.

A higher or lower P/E often reflects what investors expect for future growth and how much risk they see in those earnings. Stronger expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher “normal” P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one.

Mastercard currently trades on a P/E of 29.84x. That is above both the Diversified Financial industry average of 17.26x and the peer average of 17.98x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio metric, which estimates what a more tailored P/E might look like based on Mastercard’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks, is 19.66x.

This Fair Ratio approach can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for Mastercard’s own characteristics rather than assuming all companies should share the same multiple. Comparing the Fair Ratio of 19.66x with the current P/E of 29.84x suggests the shares trade above this tailored range.

Result: OVERVALUED
